title: Sets and maps

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Sets and maps; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Sets and maps
sources.claim: A set represents unique membership, while a map associates unique keys with values; neither structure is chosen merely because it can hold several items.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A set represents unique membership, while a map associates unique keys with values; neither structure is chosen merely because it can hold several items.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A set of completed badge identifiers answers whether a badge is present without storing duplicate completions. Your goal is to choose between sequence, set and map for a stated access requirement. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iteration; Values and types; Sequences and indexes. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A set represents unique membership, while a map associates unique keys with values; neither structure is chosen merely because it can hold several items. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Iteration order, hashability, equality, duplicate handling, and lookup complexity vary by implementation, and maps do not permit duplicate keys simultaneously. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Sets and maps are interchangeable because both usually offer fast lookup and use braces in some languages.

includes: Distinguish unique membership from key-to-value lookup.
Choose between sequence, set and map for a stated access requirement.
excludes: Iteration order, hashability, equality, duplicate handling, and lookup complexity vary by implementation, and maps do not permit duplicate keys simultaneously.

outcomes: Choose between sequence, set and map for a stated access requirement.

examples.title: Sets and maps: direct case
examples.situation: A set of completed badge identifiers answers whether a badge is present without storing duplicate completions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A set of completed badge identifiers answers whether a badge is present without storing duplicate completions.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Choose a set for membership of unique values and a map when each unique key must retrieve an associated value; state order needs separately.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Choose a set for membership of unique values and a map when each unique key must retrieve an associated value; state order needs separately.

examples.title: Sets and maps: boundary case
examples.situation: A map from learner id to current streak retrieves the streak associated with one unique learner key.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A map from learner id to current streak retrieves the streak associated with one unique learner key.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Iteration order, hashability, equality, duplicate handling, and lookup complexity vary by implementation, and maps do not permit duplicate keys simultaneously.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Sets and maps are interchangeable because both usually offer fast lookup and use braces in some languages.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose between sequence, set and map for a stated access requirement.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Sets and maps are interchangeable because both usually offer fast lookup and use braces in some languages.
misconceptions.correction: Choose a set for membership of unique values and a map when each unique key must retrieve an associated value; state order needs separately.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Sets and maps are interchangeable because both usually offer fast lookup and use braces in some languages.

analogy.example: An organized storeroom can model Sets and maps: shelves preserve order, labeled bins group fields, membership lists avoid duplicates, and indexes locate values.
usefulFor: Comparing organization by order, name, membership, lookup, and update needs
limitations: Physical storage hides concrete complexity, equality, hashing, mutation, allocation, and concurrency guarantees.
Iteration order, hashability, equality, duplicate handling, and lookup complexity vary by implementation, and maps do not permit duplicate keys simultaneously.

recap.summary: You should now be able to choose between sequence, set and map for a stated access requirement. Keep this boundary in view: Iteration order, hashability, equality, duplicate handling, and lookup complexity vary by implementation, and maps do not permit duplicate keys simultaneously.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Sets and maps without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Sets and maps are interchangeable because both usually offer fast lookup and use braces in some languages.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A map from learner id to current streak retrieves the streak associated with one unique learner key.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Data-structure choice”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
